title: A Comparison of LDPC and Polar Code on High Level Modulated MIMO-OFDM-EAC-NOMA System
abstract: For beyond 5G communication, Non-Orthogonal Multiple Access (NOMA) is an emerging multiplexing technique for a high number of users sharing the same source allocation. The joint of Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM) with NOMA improves the overall channel capacity and central efficiency of the system. Generally, the successive interference cancellation (SIC) method is used for detection users' power value at the NOMA decoding stage at low level of digital modulation. However, the ergodicity and comparison (EAC) scheme is an actual solution for high order modulation format such as 64-quadrature amplitude modulation (QAM) or even 256-QAM in 5G/6G Massive MIMO-NOMA wireless networks. Therefore, this paper focuses on the combination of EAC-NOMA scheme to the channel decoding algorithm of Low density parity check (LDPC) and Polar code (PC) on the 16×16 MIMO-OFDM-NOMA system. By assigning the power scheme, with different order of QAM, the higher power of the user, the lower of the attained BER as NOMA feature. For deploying channel decoding scheme, if increasing the number of MIMO antennas, the obtained BERs are decreased. Additionally, among the LDPC-BP, LDPC-LAMS and PC channel decoding algorithm, PC achieves the best system's performance. In our system, we propose to use PC-MMSE in Massive MIMO-OFDM-EAC-NOMA system due to the most effectiveness in error detection and error correction. From our simulation system, we propose the power ratio for user is 0.25: 0.75 as user 1: user 2, respectively.

title: Deploying Deep Learning to Improve the Performance of MIMO-OFDM Systems Using LDPC Channel Coding in V2X Communications
abstract: This paper focuses on the application of Deep Learning technology to improve the performance of V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) communication systems. Of the Multiple-input Multiple-output Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (MIMO-OFDM) system over a traffic environment, supported by Dedicated Short-Range Communication (DSRC) technology, we deploy channel coding algorithm of Low-density parity-check code (LDPC) for correcting error derived from the transmission media. Artificial Neural Network (ANN), an algorithm that can interpret raw data, is used to simulate real-world data. We apply ANN with layers as a mathematical model to train the path gain parameter, which thereafter examined the system performance BER of 5G channel coding MIMO-OFDM system over Rayleigh fading channel. We evaluate the BER and the throughput at different vehicle speeds of 60 km/h or 120 km/h as well as training process of 50 data samples or 250 data samples in scenarios of $\mathbf{2}\times \mathbf{2}$ or $\mathbf{4}\times \mathbf{4}$ MIMO antennas deployed DSRC technique. From the simulation results, training model for optimizing path gain has yielded positive results in terms of system performance and throughput, even at lower 50 data sample training. It can be said that the moving speeds of vehicles significantly affect the performance of DSRC systems due to the influence of the Doppler effect. After ANN training, if speeding up the velocity, the throughputs of $\mathbf{4}\times \mathbf{4}$ MIMO-OFDM system are increasing at lower data sample training.

title: Semantic Underwater Optical Wireless Communication Using Variational Autoencoders
abstract: The emergence of internet of underwater things has increased the demand for high-quality underwater image transmission across various applications, including infrastructure inspection and environmental monitoring. Although underwater wireless optical communication (UWOC) offers high data rates, it faces significant challenges due to channel effects that can severely degrade the quality of data transmission. This paper presents a novel deep learning (DL)-based semantic communication approach for underwater image transmission that exploits a variational autoencoder architecture in order to ensure robust performance against channel impairments. This proposed approach employs end-to-end training that jointly optimizes source coding and channel adaptation through neural network optimization, enabling transmission of semantically relevant content while adapting to channel variations. Extensive simulation results across different channel conditions show that our DL-driven semantic technique enables superior image reconstruction over longer transmission ranges, compared to the traditional approach of using image compression with powerful error correction coding. This demonstrates the potential of DL-powered semantic communication as a transformative solution for bandwidth-efficient, optical-based underwater image transmission.

title: Reliable Transmission Techniques for UAV-Assisted Communications in 5G Networks
abstract: unmanned aerial motors (UAVs) play a crucial position in 5G wireless networks because of their high mobility, flexible deployment, and line-of-sight (LOS) conversation capabilities. However, the reliable transmission of statistics in UAV-assisted communications nonetheless faces great challenges, together with constrained radio assets, excessive mobility, and intermittent connectivity. To cope with those challenges, numerous strategies have been proposed to ensure dependable transmission in 5G networks. One approach is cooperative communications, where a couple of UAVs paint collectively to relay records and expand insurance. This method exploits the diversity received from spatially separated transmitters, increasing the possibilities of hit statistics shipping. Another technique is multiband communications, wherein UAVs utilize a couple of frequency bands to transmit records simultaneously, growing the available bandwidth and improving the resiliency to interference. Hybrid automatic repeat request (HARQ) protocols have been brought to improve reliability by way of retransmitting blunders-susceptible statistics packets. Moreover, relay machine studying algorithms can be used to expect the first-class UAV function and top-of-the-line strength allocation for dependable data transmission. The aggregate of cooperative communications, multiband communications, HARQ protocols, and relay machine learning can appreciably enhance the reliability of information transmission in UAV-assisted communications in 5G networks, making them suitable for diverse use instances, along with catastrophe control, seek and rescue operations, and real-time video streaming.

title: A Hybrid Approach to Image Encryption for 6G Networks Using Integration of Lightweight LDPC Codes and RLWE Cryptosystem
abstract: 6G wireless communication promises ultra-reliable, high-capacity, and low-latency communication ensuring data security while maintaining efficiency. In this paper, we have explored a distinct method for safeguarding cloud-edge communication using data and images when integrating lattice-based encryption with error correcting codes. We have used Ring Learning with Errors (RLWE), a PQC scheme for key encapsulation, and combined with LDPC codes for correcting erroneous bits which provides both high security and reliability in 6G networks. While simulating, results show that the proposed system achieves a low Bit Error Rate (BER) of $2.04 \mathrm{e}-7$. Also, we have measured security as the computational hardness of key recovery for the proposed model, expressed on a scale from 0 to 250.

title: Bi-Evaluated Chase-Like Decoding for QC-MDPC Code
abstract: This paper presents a decoding algorithm for quasi-cyclic moderate-density parity-check (QC-MDPC) code. The method flips certain positions beforehand, and then a decoder for QC-MDPC code is executed. This process repeats if decoding fails. The positions to be flipped are determined using the number of unsatisfied parity checks (NUPCs) and $\beta$, which is the sum of NUPCs in suspicious positions after tentatively flipping a position. The proposed method is applied to a variant of bit-flipping (BF), and the block error rate (BLER) is evaluated through computer simulation. The result shows that the proposed method significantly reduces the BLER of the BF variant.

title: Dependable Static PPM Code Using LDPC Code
abstract: In recent years, with the increasing amount of data processed by computers, data compression technology is used to reduce data traffic and storage requirements. However, compressed data is vulnerable to errors. To address this issue, various data compression methods with error correction functions have been proposed. Among these error-correcting codes, LDPC code is notable for achieving a rate very close to the Shannon limit, the theoretical upper bound of information transmission. In this research, we propose a method to improve error correction capability when encoding data compressed by Static PPM, an extension of PPM compression, allowing partial decompression using LDPC codes. When large errors occur, Error correcting codes fail to correct them. However, this method can present multiple candidates, including large errors, during decoding. This allows the correct error to be selected from the candidates.

title: Optimizing LDPC Decoding with ML-Assisted Signal Refinement for Next-Gen Communications
abstract: Low-density parity-checks codes play a crucial role in ensuring reliable data transmission by correcting errors introduced by noisy communication channels. The study examines the performance of LDPC codes in Rayleigh and Rician fading environments, which are commonly encountered in real world wireless networks. Rayleigh fading occurs in areas with significant signal scattering, while Rician fading happens when both direct and scattered signals are present. To enhance the decoding process, this research incorporated a Machine Learning (ML) technique known as regression - polynomial and linear. The ML model is trained to predict and compensate for distortions in the received signal, making it clearer before decoding. The process starts with encoding data using LDPC codes, Followed by BPSK modulation. The modulated signal is transmitted through a Rayleigh or Rician fading channel, where noise and interference affect the transmission. Upon reception, the regression - based ML model processes the signal, reducing distortion and improving quality. Finally, the refined signal is decoded using an LDPC decoder, enhancing error correction and message recovery. BY combining LPDC codes with ML - based regression, this method significantly improves communication reliability, especially in challenging wireless conditions.

title: On the Derivative Structure of Euclidean Geometry Codes
abstract: Recently, Huang and Zhang [1], [2] introduced the derivative as a fundamental structure of cyclic codes, based on which the derivative decoding was further presented. It is well known that some cyclic codes constructed based on finite geometries form a special class of low-density parity-check (LDPC) codes, which perform well under the sum-product algorithm. In this paper, we study the derivative structure of Euclidean geometry (EG) codes, with a special interest in EG-LDPC codes. It is proved that for µ = m −2, the derivative ascendant of the extended (µ,s)th-order EG code of length 2ms is the extended (µ+1,s)th-order twofold EG code of length 2ms. As a subcode of EG-LDPC code, the derivative descendant of the extend (1,s)th-order twofold EG code is characterized in terms of the roots of the generator polynomial. The new code relationship suggests that the twofold EG code can leverage the sum-product decoding of the derivative descendant for its derivative decoding.

title: On Design and Analysis of Asynchronous Repetition Slotted ALOHA for Massive Access
abstract: This paper studies asynchronous repetition slotted ALOHA (a-RSA) for massive random access. Each user’s packet is repeated and allocated to randomly selected slots. Then, the users transmit simultaneously and arrive at the base station (BS) receiver with different delays, i.e., without user-synchronization. The BS receiver carries out an over-sampling-based operation, yielding an over-sampled signal space. We develop an iterative soft cancellation detection and decoding that exploits the interference structure of the over-sampled signal for powerful multi-user decoding. Afterwards, packet cancellation for a-RSA is utilized to solve packet collision. To characterize the performance of a-RSA, we analyze the achievable channel parameter region (ACPR) and outage probability. Further, we present an asymptotic analysis of the throughput of a-RSA system. It is demonstrated that the normalized throughput of a-RSA exceeds that of traditional RSA by about 20% ~ 30%.
